course_description:
This course will examine contemporary issues arising in the legal profession. Topics will vary from year to year, drawing on the innovative research of TC Beirne School of Law academic staff, as well as the expertise of visiting scholars and leading practitioners. Please refer to the Law School website for current topics.

This course will examine contemporary professional issues in the law of evidence, civil liability, civil litigation, ethics, and administrative tribunals.

The topic for Semester 1, 2026 is The International Legal Profession.

The course will explore the legal, professional and ethical knowledge and skills to successfully develop an international career in law.

This course looks at lawyers and how they work around the world. We will study how law businesses are organized, how they work, and character and ideology of the legal profession.

The course has three main sections:

What is a Lawyer? We will talk about different ideas of what a lawyer is. We will look at how lawyers are depicted by the media and entertainment industries, how they present themselves, what jobs they do, and how they work (alone, with others, or around the world).

Ethical Problems: Lawyers often face difficult choices about what lawful actions to take for their clients in high stakes, time pressured environments. This course is designed to develop a deeper understanding of global professional ethical challenges, to explore transnational professional regulation and practise norms and how to appropriately apply these in legal practice. The course will be an applied ethics approach where students will learn to adopt decision making models and practise applying ethical reasoning to realistic situations.

Changes in Law: We will study how providing legal services is changing. This includes how laws regulating lawyers are changing. We will see how these changes affect:

· What lawyers believe about professional practice and how they see themselves.

· How law businesses work (from big law firms, lawyers who help the public, company lawyers, and new types of legal services).

· The diverse and rapidly developing roles of lawyers.

This course will help students think carefully about:

· The diverse legal profession.

· How globalisation and technology are changing legal services.

· What it means to be a lawyer and how to do this effectively.

· What kind of lawyer you want to be.

LAWS7725 is an interactive course taught in seminar mode.ﾠ We will be using an interactive method of teaching at times, so you may be called upon to contribute during the seminars.ﾠ You will be expected to have read each key reading and to have thought about any assigned tutorial problem prior to class.

The reading list will be published on Blackboard by 27 January 2026.

You are encouraged to turn your phones off during class, and to limit use of your laptops when we are engaging in discussion.

The course will be taught by Professors John Swinson and Francesca Bartlett, and Bridget Burton from the UQ Law Pro Bono Centre.

Intensive Mode

This course will be taught in intensive mode, from 2 February 2026 to 6 February 2026. There will be approximately 4 to 5 hours of seminars each day, and you will be expected to attend each seminar in person. There will be a final seminar on Saturday 14 February for group assessment presentation which you must attend in person.

course_aims:
Education Mission Statement

The mission of the School of Law is to educate you in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

learning_outcomes.description: State and accurately explain the underlying drivers, macro forces and key concepts that you appreciate the reasons for the growing importance and polarisation.
Demonstrate that you can describe and critically analyse the taxonomy.
Identify and explore the risks and opportunities for businesses, including critical analysis of real-life case studies.
Formulate, share and assist in implementing a strategy in response to risks and opportunities.
Apply your acquired knowledge of the key concepts effectively, in a variety of contexts and settings (different corporate structures, industries and jurisdictions).

assessment_details.title: Group Presentation
assessment_details.task_description: Group presentation 40% (10 minutes plus approximately 5 minutes Q&A) By Day Three, you will form a small group (3-5 students). You will be given opportunities for group formation during the seminars on Day Three, and you may choose your own members. Your final assessment task will be a group presentation. Topic choice Your group will choose a topic from a list of provided topics. During the seminars we will discuss current laws, but also note areas where the law is inadequate, or is failing to address relevant issues. We will also discuss current and emerging areas of law reform. This task gives you the opportunity to choose an area of law, conduct research, and present your ideas as to how the law could achieve better outcomes for clients and society. You are encouraged to discuss your proposed topic with the course staff. Due date The group presentation will take place on Saturday 14 February 2026. In class, you will be shown how to book a session for your group presentation. Format Each oral presentation session will be 3 hours, to allow up to 10 to 12 groups to present. All group members must be present for the session. Your group will be presenting to the course coordinator and a small group of fellow students. You may use a single page handout to support your in-person presentation. Powerpoint presentations are not allowed. Group input All group members must contribute to selecting, developing, researching, and presenting the topic. The oral component of the presentation may be given by a single group member or some/all of the members. However, all group members must be prepared to answer questions during the Q&A component. Every group member will receive the same mark, unless a case is made otherwise. Two assessors will review the presentation (in-person or a recording) to provide moderation of grades. Resources to support your group work will be provided on the course BlackBoard and discussed in class. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
In-person oral presentation.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If you are seriously affected by illness or other extenuating circumstances, please contact the course coordinator as soon as possible to discuss options.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessment_details.title: Written Reflection
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Within five days of your oral presentation, you will submit an INDIVIDUAL reflections on (a) how the what you have learnt in class will impact how you undertake your career; and (b) your experiences undertaking the group oral presentation (including reflection on your topic choice, your contribution to the work, and the group work process). Further guidance will be provided in class. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Reflections must be submitted electronically via the submission link on the course Blackboard site.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
The maximum extension for an assessment item due within the semester / teaching period in which the course is offered is 7 days. In exceptional circumstances, an extension may be approved for a further 7 days. Where a student is incapacitated for a period longer than 14 days, they are advised to apply for removal of course.
A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply . Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .
Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.
A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time . Requests for extensions received after the application due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Decision-Maker Tables in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to determine whether to accept the request for consideration.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Penalties for Late Submission
The following penalties apply to late submission of an assessment item, assuming that an extension has not been approved, and that late submission is academically possible (e.g., it does not delay feedback to a cohort, or interfere with course delivery).
First 7 periods of 24 hours (or part thereof) - 10% per 24 hours of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item. For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks.
More than 7 periods of 24 hours - 100%.

assessment_details.title: Research Paper
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: A choice of research questions will be given, in relation to current issues in the international legal profession. Students will be required to prepare a research paper on a topic selected from those questions. The word limit for the essay is a maximum of 2,200 words, and any material beyond that will not be read. By way of clariﬁcation, this word count: includes headings; does not include text in the header or word count; and does not include footnotes - however footnotes should be used for citation purposes only. Any substantive text that appears in the footnote will not be read and will not contribute to your mark. For style and referencing, students should refer to the Australian Guide to Legal Citation. Use of AI This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use Artificial Intelligence (AI) and/or Machine Translation (MT) technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. Usage of AI is allowed in this assessment piece, but must be done within the indications and constraints indicated on Blackboard at the beginning of the course. These include (typically in the assessment appendix) the prompts utilised to query AI, the responses provided by the AI, and evidence of critical analysis of text-generated AI, before usage in the assessment. Moreover, usage of AI needs to be adequately referenced in the assessment. These guidelines are provided because, to pass this assessment, student will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written independent of AI tools. A failure to use AI according to the guidelines above (and detailed on Blackboard at the beginning of the course) may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy . Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: What is a lawyer? This introductory section examines the concept of the lawyer as a legal professional within contemporary Australian and comparative contexts. It explores the historical evolution of the legal profession, the institutional structures that shape legal practice, and the regulatory frameworks governing admission, professional conduct, and accountability. Attention is given to the multiple roles lawyers occupy—as advocates, advisers, officers of the court, and participants in the administration of justice—and to how these roles are shaped by ethical obligations, professional norms, and public expectations. The section also critically engages with competing theoretical perspectives on lawyering, including conceptions of the lawyer as a neutral technician, a moral agent, and a social actor with responsibilities extending beyond client service. It invites students to reflect on questions of professional identity, independence, and power, as well as the lawyer’s role in advancing access to justice, the rule of law, and the public interest. Through this inquiry, students are encouraged to situate their own professional aspirations within a broader understanding of what it means to be a lawyer in a complex, pluralistic, and evolving legal environment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Globalisation of the Profession This section considers that global aspects of the legal profession. We will consider the roles of lawyers working in an international context such as lawyers working: in a large international law firm, in-house in an international corporate legal department, for government, for an international charity, and for an international human rights or international non-government organisation. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05

International Law Firm Legal and Organisational Structures We will examine the organisational structures of international law firms, the benefits and disadvantages of each structure, and how these structures impact lawyer behaviour. For example we will consider: Swiss Verein structures Global "one firm" structures Partnerships of partnerships Corporate structures Strategic Alliances Marketing networks Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05

Economics of Law firms and impact on lawyer behaviour This section will consider the law firm as a business, how law firms make profits, and how profits are distributed. We will review the "eat what you kill" and lockstep partner compensation models. We will examine what PPEP and RPEP, how these are calculated, and what impact this has on lawyer behaviour. Learning outcomes: L03, L04

The Digital Lawyer - Impact of technology – alternative models for the delivery of legal services This section considers how new technologies, such as AI and digital legal services, and new business models, such as legal process outsourcing and globalised service delivery, will impact the practice of law in an international environment. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04, L05

The legal assistance sector - community legal services, legal aid, civil society, and pro bono legal services This section examines the legal assistance sector encompassing community legal centres, legal aid commissions, civil society organisations, and pro bono legal services. It explores the institutional roles, funding arrangements, and regulatory frameworks that shape the delivery of legal assistance to individuals and communities experiencing disadvantage. Particular attention is given to the interaction between state-funded services, non-profit and community-based providers, and the private legal profession, as well as the systemic pressures created by unmet legal need, resource constraints, and growing demand for legal support. The section also critically analyses the contribution of the legal assistance sector to access to justice, law reform, and the protection of rights. Students are encouraged to consider the ethical, professional, and policy dimensions of legal assistance work, including prioritisation of services, collaboration across sectors, and the role of pro bono practice within a broader justice ecosystem. Through engagement with contemporary challenges and reform debates, the section situates the legal assistance sector within wider discussions about the social responsibilities of lawyers, the rule of law, and the capacity of legal institutions to respond to inequality and structural injustice. Learning outcomes: L04, L05

Human rights and human rights defenders This section introduces international human rights law as a normative and institutional framework governing the protection of fundamental rights and freedoms at the global and regional levels. It examines the sources of international human rights law, including treaties, customary international law, and soft law instruments, and the roles of key actors such as the United Nations, regional human rights bodies, states, and non-state actors. The section situates international human rights norms within broader political, cultural, and legal contexts, and considers the relationship between international human rights obligations and domestic legal systems, including Australia’s engagement with international human rights mechanisms. The section also focuses on the role of human rights defenders in advancing, monitoring, and enforcing human rights in diverse international settings. It explores the legal protections afforded to human rights defenders, the risks and challenges they face—including repression, criminalisation, and violence—and the responsibilities of states and international institutions to ensure their safety and effectiveness. Students are encouraged to critically engage with contemporary case studies and debates, reflecting on the ethical responsibilities of lawyers, advocates, and civil society actors in supporting human rights work and responding to violations in an increasingly complex and contested global environment. Learning outcomes: L02, L04

Cultural Intelligence in Legal Practice This section introduces cultural intelligence as a core professional capability in contemporary legal practice. It examines how culture shapes legal institutions, communication, decision-making, and client–lawyer relationships, with particular attention to Australia’s multicultural society and its engagement with Indigenous peoples and diverse global legal systems. The section highlights the impact of cultural assumptions and power dynamics on legal processes and outcomes, and the importance of culturally responsive and reflective lawyering. The section also develops practical and ethical dimensions of cultural intelligence, including cross-cultural communication, professional judgment, and collaborative practice in domestic and international legal contexts. Students are encouraged to critically reflect on their own positionality and professional responsibilities, and to consider how culturally informed legal practice contributes to fairness, access to justice, and effective legal service delivery in an increasingly interconnected legal environment. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Professionalism in Global Practice We will examine in this section the regulations that impact the practice of law in various jurisdictions, including how government regulation impacts the practice of law across international boundries and the establishment of global law firm. Ethical issues will also be considered and discussed. Learning outcomes: L05
